ai-memory.description: Long-term project memory, handoffs, and durable annotations for agent workflows
ai-memory.setupHint: Start the ai-memory server, then point clients at http://127.0.0.1:49374/mcp or a remote /mcp endpoint. Add an Authorization header when bearer auth is enabled.
ai-memory.purpose: Persistent project memory that survives agent sessions, enabling cross-session context handoff and durable knowledge capture
ai-memory.preferredUse: Use for cross-session context handoff, durable project knowledge, and agent-to-agent memory sharing. Essential for multi-agent workflows that need shared state.
examples: Storing architectural decisions as durable wiki pages
Handing off context between agent sessions via memory_handoff_accept
Querying prior decisions with memory_query before proposing new work
Auto-improving knowledge base with memory_auto_improve at session end
antiExamples: Storing transient conversation state better kept in session context
Replacing a proper database for structured application data
Using as a general-purpose key-value store
ai-memory.securityNotes: Runs as a local HTTP server. When exposed remotely, add an Authorization header and use HTTPS. The server stores data in ~/.ai-memory/ by default. Bearer auth is supported but not enforced by default.
ai-memory.adapterOutputDifferences: Compiles to type:remote (OpenCode) or type:http (Claude Code) with URL and optional headers. Copilot emits type:sse. OMP and Antigravity emit serverUrl. All adapters preserve the URL-backed shape; no stdio command is generated.

filesystem.purpose: Read and write local files through MCP, enabling agent-driven file operations without shell access
filesystem.preferredUse: Use for file operations that need structured MCP tool access (read_file, write_file, list_directory). Prefer CLI for bulk operations or when shell access is available.
examples: Reading a specific file by path with read_file
Writing generated code to a new file with write_file
Listing directory contents to discover project structure
Searching files by pattern with search_files
antiExamples: Bulk file operations (use shell commands instead)
Git operations (use gh CLI or native git)
filesystem.securityNotes: Scoped to the project directory by default (the '.' argument). Changing the directory argument expands the access scope. Never run with a root-level or unrestricted path.
filesystem.adapterOutputDifferences: Compiles to type:local (OpenCode) or stdio command (Claude Code, Copilot). All adapters emit the npx command with args. No URL-based variant exists.

ripgrep.purpose: High-performance regex code search across the project, powered by ripgrep
ripgrep.preferredUse: Use for regex-based code search when you need structured results. Prefer native rg CLI for simple searches to save tokens.
examples: Searching for a function definition across the codebase with regex patterns
Finding all files matching a glob pattern with list-files
Complex multi-pattern searches where structured results help
antiExamples: Simple substring searches (use rg directly via shell)
File existence checks (use find or shell test)
ripgrep.securityNotes: Runs npx mcp-ripgrep which downloads the package on first use. Searches are scoped to the project directory by the MCP server. No network access beyond initial package fetch.
ripgrep.adapterOutputDifferences: Compiles to type:local (OpenCode) or stdio command (Claude Code, Copilot). All adapters emit the npx command with args. No URL-based variant exists.

codegraph.description: Code knowledge graph for semantic exploration
codegraph.setupHint: Initialize your repository index by running: codegraph init
codegraph.purpose: Semantic code search and knowledge graph exploration for understanding code structure and relationships
codegraph.preferredUse: Use for semantic code search, understanding code relationships, and exploring code structure beyond text matching. Essential for large codebases where grep is insufficient.
examples: Finding all callers of a function with codegraph_search
Understanding code architecture through relationship queries
Discovering related files across the project structure
antiExamples: Simple text search (use ripgrep or rg instead)
File read operations (use filesystem MCP or shell)
When the codegraph index is stale (re-run codegraph init first)
codegraph.securityNotes: Runs a local server that indexes the repository. The index is stored in .codegraph/ and can be large for big repos. No network access. Requires bun to be installed.
codegraph.adapterOutputDifferences: Compiles to type:local (OpenCode) or stdio command (Claude Code, Copilot). All adapters emit the codegraph serve --mcp command. No URL-based variant exists.

obsidian.description: Obsidian vault read/write access for persistent knowledge
obsidian.setupHint: Ensure the 'Advanced URI' plugin is installed in your Obsidian vault and enabled.
obsidian.purpose: Read, write, and search Obsidian vault notes through MCP for persistent personal knowledge management
obsidian.preferredUse: Use for reading and writing Obsidian notes when structured MCP access is needed. Prefer the ob CLI for bulk operations or when MCP is not available.
examples: Reading a specific note by path with read_note
Creating a new note with write_note for durable knowledge capture
Searching notes by content with search_notes
Listing all notes in a vault directory
antiExamples: Bulk note imports or exports (use ob CLI directly)
When the vault is not local (Obsidian sync may cause conflicts)
Binary attachments or media files (use filesystem MCP instead)
obsidian.securityNotes: Requires the Advanced URI plugin in Obsidian. The ob CLI communicates with the local Obsidian instance. Vault access is scoped to configured vaults. No network access beyond local Obsidian.
obsidian.adapterOutputDifferences: Compiles to type:local (OpenCode) or stdio command (Claude Code, Copilot). All adapters emit the ob mcp command. No URL-based variant exists.

ai-jail.description: Sandbox wrapper for AI coding agents with per-project path constraints
ai-jail.installHint: brew tap akitaonrails/tap && brew install ai-jail

ai-memory.description: Thin client and installer for the ai-memory project memory server

gh.description: GitHub CLI for repository, issue, and PR operations (never pushes without explicit user approval)

codegraph.description: Code knowledge graph for semantic exploration

ob.description: Obsidian vault read/write access for persistent knowledge
